Step-by-Step Build Guide

import { Aside, Steps } from ‘@astrojs/starlight/components’;

This guide walks you through assembling a complete PolyKybd Split72 from parts. Have all required parts ready before you start.

A build video is also available: Watch on YouTube

  1. Prepare optional rotary encoder (if using)

    If you are using an Alps EC11 encoder, place it on the PCB and temporarily fit the plate on top to verify it clears. Add a strip of Kapton tape over the legs if they come close to the aluminum plate.

    If you are using an EVQWGD001 encoder, clip away the last pin before fitting it. Do not solder it yet — fit the plate first to check alignment.

  2. Prepare optional Pimoroni trackball (if using)

    Solder the header pins without the plastic distance spacer (push the spacer off with pliers after soldering, or insert pins from the front and clip the plastic from the back). Apply Kapton tape to the back of the trackball board, then solder it to the PCB flush to the front surface (expect 1–2mm gap). Cover the front of the trackball with Kapton tape or other insulating material to prevent shorts against the aluminum plate.

  3. Prepare optional Cirque trackpad (if using)

    Remove R1 from the back of the trackpad PCB to switch it from SPI to I2C. Connect the 12-pin FPC cable to the trackpad (note the pin 1 marker). Place the trackpad in its 3D-printed holder and secure with a small amount of adhesive. Do not connect to the PCB yet — that comes in step 6.

  4. Install the status display

    Insert the 0.96” status display into its holder (bend the holder back and let it snap around the display). Connect the FPC cable to the socket on the PCB and lock it by lowering the brown flap on the FPC socket.

    If you are not installing a status display, fit the dummy cover in its place.

  5. Place the spacer and fit the plate

    Put the spacer on top of the PCB. Slide the plate down over the assembly, guiding the status display holder (or dummy) through the plate cutout from the back side. The plate rests on the spacer. Temporarily insert two screws at diagonal corners to align the plate and PCB.

  6. Install the Cirque trackpad (if using)

    Feed the FPC cable through the slot in the plate, push the trackpad holder into place (press-fit, no glue needed), then insert the FPC into the trackpad socket on the PCB and lock it by lowering the flap. Verify pin 1 alignment.

  7. Assemble all 72 keys

    For each key:

    • Place the 3D-printed stem onto the key switch
    • Thread the display FPC cable through the LED slit of the switch
    • Align the display against the stem
    • Press the clear keycap cover over the stem — the display will slide into the stem pocket as you press it down

    Do not pre-bend the FPC cable. Forcing a bend risks damaging it.

    Repeat for all 72 keys.

  8. Insert corner key switches

    Insert one assembled switch at each corner first to lock the alignment of plate and PCB:

    • Feed the FPC into the PCB slot
    • Push the switch straight down into the hot-swap socket
    If you feel resistance before the switch bottoms out, **stop**. Forcing it risks detaching the hot-swap socket from the PCB. Pull the switch out, check that both pins are straight, and try again.

    You can verify correct insertion by checking the back of the PCB — both pins should be visible through the socket. One pin missing means the switch needs to be reseated.

  9. Insert all remaining switches

    Insert the remaining 68 switches the same way. After each switch, briefly check the back of the PCB to confirm both pins are seated.

  10. Connect all display FPC cables

    For each of the 72 displays:

    • Use tweezers or a toothpick to lift the small locking flap on the FPC socket
    • Insert the FPC cable into the socket (tweezers help, but fingers work too)
    • Lower the flap to lock

    If a cable is too short, gently press the key switch down to release a little more slack.

  11. Insert hex nuts (case r4+)

    If your case is revision r4 or later, press the 8× M3 hex nuts into the recesses in the case corners before installing the PCB assembly.

  12. Flash the firmware

    Before sealing the case, do a full function check. If you have not yet flashed the firmware:

    • Build or download the firmware from github.com/thpoll83/qmk_firmware
    • Hold the BOOT button on the PCB, then connect the USB-C cable while holding it
    • Release the button — the board mounts as a USB drive
    • Copy the .uf2 file to the USB drive

    See Flashing the Firmware for full details.

  13. Function check: displays

    Connect the USB-C cable (you do not need to connect the second half yet). After one or two seconds, all 72 key displays should show characters.

    If any displays are blank, disconnect the cable, release the lock on that display’s FPC socket, re-seat the cable, lock it again, and reconnect.

  14. Function check: keys

    While still connected, press every key once. Each pressed key should invert its display content (white-on-black becomes black-on-white).

    If a key does not respond, one or both switch pins are likely bent. Remove the switch, straighten the pins, and re-insert.

  15. Place assembly in case

    Once all displays and keys work, lower the assembly into the case. Start on the inner edge (where the bridge USB-C socket is) and ease the outer edge in with a gentle push.

  16. Fasten the screws

    Insert and tighten the 4× M3×10 hex screws on each half. Do not overtighten — snug is enough.

  17. Connect the two halves

    Connect the short USB-C to USB-C cable between the two halves.

  18. Connect to your computer

    Plug the USB-C cable from the left half to your computer. You are done!

Congratulations

Section titled “Congratulations”

If all displays light up and all keys respond, your PolyKybd Split72 is ready to use.

Next steps:

  • Install PolyKybdHost for context-aware per-key overlays
  • Customize your keymap
  • Set up unicode input
